next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

Yusuf hadn't left the calibration bay since the crash. He'd been in there fourteen hours now, maybe fifteen. He'd stopped checking the clock around midnight, when the log analysis ate whatever part of his attention had been keeping time. The mug of coffee on the workstation ledge had gone cold hours ago. He moved it aside every time he reached for the secondary display and moved it back when he was done, a small rhythm that kept his hands occupied while the data on the screen got steadily worse.

The bay's constant sixty-hertz hum, the processors, the cooling system, the background drone of a building full of machinery keeping bodies tuned, had shifted in his perception sometime around four in the morning. It no longer sounded like infrastructure. It sounded like something running a fever.

He scrolled through another block of propagation logs. Outside the bay's single window, snow fell in the predawn dark. The facility was under restricted-operations protocol: no training sessions, no calibrations, no scheduled maintenance. Everything was offline until his team could map the extent of the corruption. Forty-three athletes were in their quarters or the common areas or the cafeteria, waiting for someone to tell them whether their bodies were safe. Yusuf was supposed to be that someone.

The pattern was clear. He'd been hoping it wouldn't be.

Every athlete who had connected to the calibration network in the past thirty-two days showed firmware irregularities. Not all were catastrophic. Kai's blackout was the worst case, a full cascade that wiped sensory processin...
open_hooks_to_carry: Full sensory blackout at the 140m mark—no sight, no proprioception, no sound, but the synthetic grafts keep driving the legs
The crash into the wall at full speed: shoulder dislocation, facial lacerations, broken wrist
Yusuf finds cascading corruption in the shared calibration network—it behaves like a virus
prev_tail_excerpt:
...Petrov at seven tomorrow morning, sprint conditioning. Two gymnasts Thursday afternoon. Friday, Kai Brennan had requested a neural coaching evaluation for his proprioception issues. She left his name on the screen longer than the others.

Next came Tomás's report. Minor sprain, right knee. Contributing factor: altitude adjustment, incomplete cardiovascular acclimatization. Recommendation: two days off hurdle work, light conditioning permitted. The trainer's summary was already locked into the medical file before she finished reading. Her coaching note dropped underneath it. The target edit would slide into the nightly audit queue with every other threshold tweak and missed biometric warning unless Petra chose to go looking.

Her forearms still remembered the jolt when Tomás hit the track, not the whole scene, just the jolt in her palms, wrists, and elbows, plus the hot scrape along the cheek where his face had gone down. The feed left pieces behind.

By tomorrow, Hana would replace it. One of the gymnasts might, if Hana didn't, and Kai on Friday could do it if the others failed. A newer body would push this one out. That was the plan.

Nothing urgent sat in her email. Outside, the ridge had gone fully black. In the window she was only an outline, which was better.

The wrist mount waited on the desk. She picked it up, turned it over, thumbed the warm contacts, and pressed it to her pulse point without connecting it. The metal sat dead on her skin. She held it there through four breaths, until the fresh pressure mark matched the old one, then set it down and went to dinner.
state_deltas_required: The virus seizes Kai's senses while his body keeps running at full sprint speed
From theoretical vulnerability to visceral physical horror—the threat is real and inside their bodies
